chunk_summary: Children with severe neurodisability face high risks of micronutrient deficiencies and poor bone health, requiring annual surveillance, targeted lab panels, and preventive measures like vitamin D correction, calcium optimization, and mobility support.
learning_objectives: Understand the risk factors for micronutrient deficiencies and poor bone health in children with severe neurodisability.
Identify appropriate laboratory tests and screening intervals for high-risk children.
Learn preventive strategies for optimizing bone health, including dietary and mobility interventions.
Recognize the importance of tailored approaches in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: Children with neurodisability are at higher risk of micronutrient deficiency due to low intake, selective diets, and anticonvulsant use.
Annual screening is recommended for high-risk children, with intensified monitoring during catch-up feeding or after repeated infections.
A clinic-ready lab panel includes CBC with iron status, vitamin D, calcium, phosphate, and potentially B12, folate, zinc, and copper.
Low-trauma fractures warrant evaluation of calcium-phosphate balance and secondary causes, with DXA scans considered where feasible.
Tube feeding improves nutrient delivery but does not automatically address bone health risks without monitoring and supplementation.
In resource-limited settings, prioritize hemoglobin, iron, vitamin D, and calcium-phosphate testing during scheduled visits.
reference_quote: Children with cerebral palsy are at increased risk of osteoporosis due to reduced mobility, poor nutrition, and anticonvulsant use. (Source: https://www.aacpdm.org/publications/care-pathways/osteoporosis-in-cerebral-palsy)
